dialogue.utterance: Dispatch for Unit 3 responding to a 79-year-old female with breathing problems at a [CITY] residence.
Hello, I'm Morgan from EMS. What led you to call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath. It's been like this all night.
Beatrice, could you tell me your full name and current location?
You have your eyes open and are making good contact with us. That's a good sign.
My name is Beatrice, and I'm at my living room in [CITY]. I'm having trouble breathing.
If you can, squeeze my finger. You're able to move well. Good motor response.
Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15 (Eye=4, Verbal=5, Motor=6). You are alert and oriented now.
Can you tell me the current date and year please, Beatrice?>
Today is 2019. Probably May or April.
Can you tell me your reason for calling 911 again?
I can't breathe well. It started all night and has gotten worse today.
Your airway is currently patent and maintained without obstruction.
Let me count your respirations to assess breathing. Okay, let's go.
Your breathing rate is 26 breaths per minute. That's quite fast for someone unwell.
Resp: 26. Still elevated compared to normal breathing rates.
May we listen to your lungs for abnormal sounds?>
I'm hearing wheezing on both inhalation and exhalation in all lung fields. Sounds are audible without the stethoscope.
Who would like to take the pulse? Can we have the BP setup as well?
Please let me take a pulse reading now. Got it: 67 beats per minute and regular in rhythm.
67 BPM, strong and regular.
Now, I'm going to measure your blood pressure. You'll feel a brief squeeze on your arm, okay?
191 over 99 millimeters of mercury. It's elevated, but we monitor changes over time.
I am now placing the pulse oximeter on your finger to assess oxygen saturation levels. Ready?
SpO2 reading is 96%. That remains within the acceptable range for oxygen saturation.
EKG shows a sinus rhythm-no significant dysrhythmias or other findings at this time.
We will apply capnography now to continuously monitor your ventilation depth and effectiveness.
Bystander, can you help us with allergies and medical history?>
She has known COPD and type 1 diabetes. She's been on insulin for years.
For how long has she experienced these conditions?>
COPD for over ten years and the diabetes for a few years as well.
Can you let us know which medications she's currently taking, Beatrice?>
She uses insulin and inhaled medications, but neither is available right now, she says.
She is allergic to Penicillin and Codeine with Guaifenesin for respiratory use.
On a scale of 0 to 10, is there any actual pain you're experiencing?>
No pain whatsoever. I just can't breathe properly. It's very uncomfortable with the breathing.
Observing for additional symptoms now. There is generalized weakness noted. Is this something new to you?>
It is new. Usually I feel mostly tired with my COPD, but not this kind of weakness.
Bystander administered 15 liters per minute oxygen via NRB-how much does it help you?>
It helps a little. Still feel tightness in the chest, but better now with the mask than not.
We will give you CPAP to assist breathing, which should support your respiratory efforts.
CPAP is now fully in place and active to help keep your airway open during breathing.
We will administer nebulized Albuterol 5mg and Atrovent 0.5mg for bronchodilation now.
Then we will give a 10mg IM injection of Dexamethasone in the upper left deltoid muscle to reduce respiratory inflammation now.
Earlier your glucose was zero, so I'm taking a new test now. Result is 124 mg/dL, which is better.
124 mg/dL now. It's stabilizing, and trending in a safer range for your body's needs now.
Based on the findings, we will follow protocol 3-11 for COPD exacerbation and associated respiratory distress in the field.
Let's reassess now. Pulse is 128 and respiratory rate at 28. Is that expected given the findings?>
Blood pressure is now 120 over 85. We can accept that as a normal and documented value at this time.
SpO2 improved to 98%. That's an improvement but remains ongoing during transport monitoring.
Breathing is still quite difficult. And no, there is still no pain. Is there any change from before?>
Tightness the same as before. Still no real pain. No new symptom.
We maintain CPAP in alignment with protocol for continued respiratory support and monitoring.
Respiratory rate dropped back to 11 breaths a minute after interventions.
Resp is 11. Noted as a stable value now after initial improvement.
Blood pressure is now 130 over 87.
BP: 130/87. Stable and acceptable.
Pulse is now 127, still regular and strong.
Can you raise your arms now for strength evaluation.
Fine, I can.
Looks good. No signs of nuchal rigidity. Neck is soft with no destructive signs.
Skin color is good, but generalized weakness was observed earlier. No other dermatological signs.
We're applying the 4-lead EKG now for continuous monitoring of your cardiac baseline.
EKG patch applied and leads in place for monitoring field events now.
Time to power up the monitor now for consistent and objective EKG readings.
Let's try to place an IV in an arm or leg for potential emergency medications if necessary.
IV placement was unsuccessful after three attempts due to poor peripheral access.
Will you permit transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for the COPD flare-up, or is there another wish?
Captain, take me to [CITY]. There I know the staff from prior care.
Let's move you to the stretcher. You appear stable enough for transfer during transport.
Patient is being placed on the stretcher now for secured transport.
You are now securely strapped on all points. All preparations are complete for transport.
We'll maintain Fowlers position during transport for optimal breathing assistance with CPAP.
CPAP remains on and is helping support breathing as intended by protocol.
Dexamethasone IM was given earlier. No need for re-dose at this time.
Patient condition is continuing to follow the COPD exacerbation protocol.
Thank you, Beatrice, for your cooperation during the move. CPAP will stay active for safe support.
IVO access considered, but viable access never achieved with three failed attempts.
Pulse shows 127, BP at 130/87, and oxygen saturation remained at 96%. All held steady.
No EKG abnormalities noted. Cardiac baseline behind appears stable.
CPAP continues as active aid for breathing per protocol.
Albuterol, Atrovent already administered for respiratory management in the field.
We are nearing the hospital. Any development in breathing or discomfort since initiation of CPAP?
Still feel some difficulty but nothing new or worse for now. Everything's manageable.
Hospital has been updated with the patient's findings and interventions done prior to arrival.
Let the receiving staff know a "hot case" for COPD exacerbation is en route now.
Patient is secured and oriented in Fowlers during the transit.
SpO2 still at 96%, pulse at 127, and EKG is trending normally with no change detected.
Arrival is in five minutes. I recommend you continue breathing slowly and deeply for airway comfort.
Field assessment continues to support the COPD flare-up under standard EMS care workflow.
Transport is proceeding smoothly with all systems stable so far.
All readings unchanged-127 BPM, 130/87 BP, and 96% saturation-clinical intervention no longer needed.
